name: Soldering a Wire Join
description: Creating a durable, conductive electrical joint by melting solder onto twisted copper wires. Requires safety gear and temperature monitoring.

steps.name: Strip and Twist Wires
steps.description: Strip approximately 1 inch of insulation from two multi-strand wires, then twist the copper strands together tightly in a Lineman splice.
steps.voice_guideline: Welcome to your Soldering Coach. Step 1: Strip and Twist Wires. Strip about 1 inch of insulation, then braid the copper strands together tightly.
steps.illustrative_image_prompt: Vector drawing of two colored insulated wires stripped to expose shiny twisted copper strands in a tight Lineman splice.

checklist.title: Bare Copper Exposed
checklist.description: At least 3/4 inch of bare copper is visible on both wires

checklist.title: Splice Twisted
checklist.description: The copper strands are braided tightly together with no loose loose threads

failure_conditions.description: Wires twisted too close to insulated rubber
failure_conditions.trigger_description: Insulation is overlapping with splice area, risking burning plastic
failure_conditions.mitigation_instruction: Ensure a small gap exists between the twisted copper and the rubber insulation so you don't melt the casing.

steps.name: Heat the Copper Joint
steps.description: Clean your soldering iron tip, apply a tiny bit of fresh solder to 'tin' the tip, and hold the flat side of the tip directly against the underside of the copper splice.
steps.voice_guideline: Step 2: Heat the Copper Joint. Clean and tin your soldering iron tip, then hold it flat against the underside of the copper wires to transfer heat.
steps.illustrative_image_prompt: Vector drawing of a hot soldering iron tip placed against the bottom side of twisted copper wires, with heat transfer waves.

checklist.title: Iron Tip Tinned
checklist.description: Soldering iron tip is clean and shiny with fresh solder

checklist.title: Iron Touching Copper
checklist.description: The hot tip is making direct, flat contact with the copper wires to heat them up

failure_conditions.description: User gripping the metal barrel instead of the plastic handle
failure_conditions.trigger_description: User's fingers are detected too close to the hot heating element
failure_conditions.mitigation_instruction: DANGER: Move your fingers back immediately! Only hold the rubber or plastic insulated handle of the iron.

steps.name: Flow the Solder
steps.description: Press the solder wire against the TOP of the heated copper wire joint (not the iron tip itself). Let the heated copper melt the solder so it flows into the gaps.
steps.voice_guideline: Step 3: Flow the Solder. Press your solder wire against the top of the hot copper wires, letting the heated copper melt the solder natively.
steps.illustrative_image_prompt: Vector drawing showing solder wire pressed against the top of the hot copper join, flowing and melting evenly through the wire strands.

checklist.title: Solder on Copper
checklist.description: Solder wire is applied to the copper strands

checklist.title: Solder Flowed
checklist.description: Solder has melted and fully coated the copper strands evenly

failure_conditions.description: Solder was melted directly on the iron tip rather than letting the heated copper wire melt it
failure_conditions.trigger_description: Solder touching iron tip directly, causing a weak cold joint
failure_conditions.mitigation_instruction: Do not touch the solder directly to the iron tip. Touch it to the heated copper wires on the opposite side so they melt it.

failure_conditions.description: Soldering iron has been in contact with the wire for over 8 seconds continuously
failure_conditions.trigger_description: Time touching exceeds threshold, risking burning copper and melting wire insulation
failure_conditions.mitigation_instruction: Remove the iron tip now! You are overheating the joint. Let it cool down before trying again.

steps.name: Cool and Inspect Joint
steps.description: Remove the soldering iron and solder wire. Keep the joint completely motionless for 5 seconds while it solidifies. Inspect the finished joint.
steps.voice_guideline: Final Step: Cool and Inspect. Remove both the iron and solder. Keep the joint completely motionless while it cools to solid silver.
steps.illustrative_image_prompt: Vector drawing of a shiny, silver, solidified electrical joint cooling under a magnifying glass.

checklist.title: Iron Removed
checklist.description: The soldering iron heating element has been removed from the work area

checklist.title: Solder Solidified
checklist.description: The solder has cooled and the wires are held rigid

failure_conditions.description: A grainy, dull, or fractured joint surface indicating movement during cooling
failure_conditions.trigger_description: Dull grey look or cracked texture detected in wire connection
failure_conditions.mitigation_instruction: The solder finish is dull and grainy, indicating a cold joint. Re-heat the wire and let it cool completely motionless this time.
